questions.a: 99 percent of requests finish at or below that latency.
The amount of work completed per unit time.
Waiting on disk, network, or another external resource is the bottleneck.

questions.q: What is a flame graph used for?
questions.a: Visualizing where CPU time is spent in a program.
Combining multiple units of work into one operation.

questions.q: Why do averages hide performance problems?
questions.a: They smooth out slow tail requests that users actually feel.
It relates concurrency, throughput, and latency, so you can estimate system behavior.
It shows the limited speedup from optimizing only one part of a workload.

questions.q: Why do connection pools improve latency?
questions.a: They avoid repeated setup cost and reuse warmed connections.

questions.q: Why is an N+1 query pattern slow?
questions.a: It turns one logical request into many repeated round trips.

questions.q: Why does load testing need a steady methodology?
questions.a: Without controlled inputs, benchmark results are noisy and misleading.

questions.q: When should you optimize for tail latency instead of average latency?
questions.a: When user experience or SLAs are dominated by slow outliers.

questions.q: What tradeoff comes with aggressive caching?
questions.a: It reduces latency, but freshness and invalidation complexity increase.

questions.q: When is throughput more important than latency?
questions.a: When you need to process bulk work and individual request time matters less.

questions.q: Why do performance regressions often come from contention?
questions.a: Threads or requests start waiting on the same shared resource.

questions.q: What should you measure before optimizing?
questions.a: The actual bottleneck, the workload shape, and a baseline you trust.
More parallel work can improve throughput, but synchronization overhead may rise.
